## Results and analysis files

//ad.helsinki.fi/group/h30492/space_tcycif1/tCyCIF/spatial_analysis/output_data/2_spatial_metrics)

- Whole exome sequencing: To be added (TBA)\...

## Results and analysis files

[Description]{.underline}**:** The results are mainly generated on the
go. The most important part of the project is the development of the
analysis pipeline/model.

[Computers used]{.underline}**:** Currently, the analysis is mainly
performed on Venla's Laptop; if needed, we can use the workstations in
the lab.

[Paths]{.underline}**:** TBA

Analysis files (non-sensitive) can be saved in:
smb://[[ad.helsinki.fi/group/h30492/space_tcycif1/tCyCIF/SPACEjoint]{.underline}](http://ad.helsinki.fi/group/h30492/space_tcycif1/tCyCIF/SPACEjoint)

## Developed code

[Github]{.underline}:
[[https://github.com/farkkilab/SPACE]{.underline}](https://github.com/farkkilab/SPACE)

## Experiment plans

[Description]{.underline}: The project is mainly about computational
analysis; no additional wet lab experiments are planned.

## Additional key relevant files

Venla's research plan: [[Venla\'s Research
Plan]{.underline}](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1q0IcPdhi2BHFWewgv91pYCEofJQysIsQnr-UMDoEK0s/edit?usp=sharing)

## Members of the project:

Venla Kaislo (2025-)

Ziqi Kang (2025-)

# 

# Project log

## 10.03.2025

### Ziqi: Introduce the spatial metrics for the SPACEjoint project from tCyCIF

Link to find the data tables:
smb://[[ad.helsinki.fi/group/h30492/space_tcycif1/tCyCIF/spatial_analysis/output_data/2_spatial_metrics]{.underline}](http://ad.helsinki.fi/group/h30492/space_tcycif1/tCyCIF/spatial_analysis/output_data/2_spatial_metrics)

- You will also find a README.txt file in this path that contains
 > detailed descriptions for each feature.

- Ziqi is continuing to work on it to generate more features.

### Whole Exome Sequencing update: Angela submitted the request to export

Wait for the iCAN platform to approve.

Hopefully, we can get it within 1 month.

### Venla update: Reading and exploring

We discussed some future tasks for coding:

**For survival labels**

- Try to build some survival models with R just to practice

 - Plot: Kapplan-Meir plots

 - Forest plot: of the Cox regression of overall survival, etc

**With HRD or HRP**:

- Build the general models with the task of classification.

 - Plot for accuracy, etc

 - Visualize the importance of each feature

### Matilda will provide the ratio of HRD within 1-2 weeks. 

**The next meeting will be on March 24th.**

## 24.03.2025

### Update

- Try to build some survival models with R just to practice √

Members of the project:

Venla Kaislo (2025-)

Ziqi Kang (2025-)

## 10.03.2025

### Ziqi: Introduce the spatial metrics for the SPACEjoint project from tCyCIF

Link to find the data tables:

### Whole Exome Sequencing update: Angela submitted the request to export

Wait for the iCAN platform to approve.

Hopefully, we can get it within 1 month.

### Venla update: Reading and exploring

We discussed some future tasks for coding:

**For survival labels**

- Try to build some survival models with R just to practice

 - Plot: Kapplan-Meir plots

 - Forest plot: of the Cox regression of overall survival, etc

**With HRD or HRP**:

- Build the general models with the task of classification.

 - Plot for accuracy, etc

 - Visualize the importance of each feature

### Matilda will provide the ratio of HRD within 1-2 weeks. 

**The next meeting will be on March 24th.**

## 24.03.2025

### Update

- Try to build some survival models with R just to practice √

provide the ratio of HRD within 1-2 weeks. 

**The next meeting will be on March 24th.**

## 24.03.2025

### Update

- Try to build some survival models with R just to practice √
 > Perfectly finished!

- Imbalance of the Long/short PFS (wide CI in the forest plot) -\>
 > need to do sth, too many long too small short group

### Next Step

1. Integrate the **spatial metrics + ratio of HRD/HRP (test_score)**
 > into the survival model -\> see if it fits with Ziqi's
 > significance test

> You can find the spatial metrics in the:
> smb://[[ad.helsinki.fi/group/h30492/space_tcycif1/tCyCIF/spatial_analysis/output_data/2_spatial_metrics]{.underline}](http://ad.helsinki.fi/group/h30492/space_tcycif1/tCyCIF/spatial_analysis/output_data/2_spatial_metrics).
>
> The files you might need are (if sth not clear, check README.txt or
> ask Ziqi):

- Immune to TSI median distance:
 > **immune_median_distance_TSI_RCN.csv**

- Immune networks

 - Myolents (Macrophages, DCs): **Lymphonet_delaunay.csv**

 - Lymonets (B cell, CD4T, CD8T, Treg, NK):
 > **Myelonet_delaunay.csv**

2. Instead, you can use the numerical value of PFS in your clinical
 > model

3. If you have time, try to adjust the threshold of long & short PFS
 > labels -\> current threshold is 12 months, suggest using 18 months

### Suggestions 

1. Try to check the distribution of the features before the analysis,

provide the ratio of HRD within 1-2 weeks. 

**The next meeting will be on March 24th.**

## 14.04.2025

### Update & Questions for Matilda

- Unclear about which feature to include √

- Provide Ziqi's significance test (it's in the slide, Ziqi will
 > organize the results here) √

- Question for Matilda:

> ![](media/image4.png){width="5.932292213473316in"
> height="3.4920581802274717in"}

- HRD/HRP ratio

 - Can we log transform the ratio?

 - Matilda is not sure whether doing such transformation will
 > change the biological meanings of the variable. She
 > suggests first checking the absolute score and ignoring
 > the result of extremely small values (0.1). If not good we
 > could then [continue with log transform]{.underline}.

 - Why does it show differences in the trend compared with
 > HRD_result (labels) in the forest plot?

 - We shouldn't include those [2 variables at the same time
 > because they are highly relevant]{.underline} - that will
 > confuse the Hazard model. We should choose independent
 > variables.

- Why does progression have such a wide CI?

 - For overall survival, the progression is not a good predictor as

of long & short PFS
 > labels -\> current threshold is 12 months, suggest using 18 months

### Suggestions 

1. Try to check the distribution of the features before the analysis,
 > so you can find the risk of imbalance before the analysis

2. Suggested some papers to read: Missing Data and Data Imputation in
 > Clinical Data. :)

## 14.04.2025

### Update & Questions for Matilda

- Unclear about which feature to include √

- Provide Ziqi's significance test (it's in the slide, Ziqi will
 > organize the results here) √

- Question for Matilda:

> ![](media/image4.png){width="5.932292213473316in"
> height="3.4920581802274717in"}

- HRD/HRP ratio

 - Can we log transform the ratio?

 - Matilda is not sure whether doing such transformation will
 > change the biological meanings of the variable. She
 > suggests first checking the absolute score and ignoring
 > the result of extremely small values (0.1). If not good we
 > could then [continue with log transform]{.underline}.

 - Why does it show differences in the trend compared with
 > HRD_result (labels) in the forest plot?

 - We shouldn't include those [2 variables at the same time
 > because they are highly relevant]{.underline} - that will
 > confuse the Hazard model. We should choose independent
 > variables.

- Why does progression have such a wide CI?

 - For overall survival, the progression is not a good predictor as

fuse the Hazard model. We should choose independent
 > variables.

- Why does progression have such a wide CI?

 - For overall survival, the progression is not a good predictor as
 > they are too directly relevant. Instead we could use
 > **Stage**. When using the stage you could code the stage IIIA,
 > IIIB, etc into 0, 1, 2, ...

See some examples:

![](media/image15.png){width="4.102642169728784in"
height="3.4721905074365704in"}Red box: spatial metrics

![](media/image17.png){width="4.13125in" height="3.0373709536307962in"}

One of our goal is to test which molecular signature (e.g. CNV ratio, we
will receive in the future) and spatial metrics (See below, from tCycIF
image analysis) are relevant with the clinical labels. -\> This is an
important step to prove the impact of our findings :)

### Next step

**New features for spatial analysis**

- **Allimage_basic_metric.csv**: you can get the **proportion of
 > RCNs** in each patient image.

 - ![](media/image1.png){width="4.554312117235345in"
 > height="4.401042213473316in"}

 - **% RCN11, RCN3 and RCN9** -\> enrich in HRP group, **but not
 > sure with OS or PFS**

- **Allimage_spatial_metric_1**

 - **Tumor_stroma_ratio** (if high ratio -\> more tumor, this is a
 > bad condition) **& tumor_stroma_interface_length** (tell you
 > the shape of the tumor)

 - If low ratio -\> high OS or PFS

fuse the Hazard model. We should choose independent
 > variables.

- Why does progression have such a wide CI?

 - For overall survival, the progression is not a good predictor as
 > they are too directly relevant. Instead we could use
 > **Stage**. When using the stage you could code the stage IIIA,
 > IIIB, etc into 0, 1, 2, ...

See some examples:

![](media/image15.png){width="4.102642169728784in"
height="3.4721905074365704in"}Red box: spatial metrics

![](media/image17.png){width="4.13125in" height="3.0373709536307962in"}

One of our goal is to test which molecular signature (e.g. CNV ratio, we
will receive in the future) and spatial metrics (See below, from tCycIF
image analysis) are relevant with the clinical labels. -\> This is an
important step to prove the impact of our findings :)

### Next step

**New features for spatial analysis**

- **Allimage_basic_metric.csv**: you can get the **proportion of
 > RCNs** in each patient image.

 - ![](media/image1.png){width="4.554312117235345in"
 > height="4.401042213473316in"}

 - **% RCN11, RCN3 and RCN9** -\> enrich in HRP group, **but not
 > sure with OS or PFS**

- **Allimage_spatial_metric_1**

 - **Tumor_stroma_ratio** (if high ratio -\> more tumor, this is a
 > bad condition) **& tumor_stroma_interface_length** (tell you
 > the shape of the tumor)

 - If low ratio -\> high OS or PFS

ma_ratio** (if high ratio -\> more tumor, this is a
 > bad condition) **& tumor_stroma_interface_length** (tell you
 > the shape of the tumor)

 - If low ratio -\> high OS or PFS

 - Tumor_stroma_interface -\> we are not sure with OS or PFS.
 > Could be hard to normalize this value (Have a try!)

- Allimage_spatial_metric_2 **(low priority to integrate)**

 - For **each cell type**：(we don't sure which cell type will
 > contribute more -\> if you are interested in it, you can start
 > with [B cells and CD4T cells]{.underline})

 - **NND:** nearest neighbour distance, measure the
 > distribution of certain cell type, are they loose or
 > dense.

 - **cluster Density:** compute the density of clusters with
 > DBSCAN, radius = 30px, minimum of 10 cells are considered
 > as a cluster.

 - **Gini Density:** Gini coefficient for cell density
 > variation, measure the inequality of a distribution.

 - More close to 1 means the more unequal the distribution
 > of certain cell type. e.g. The unequal distribution of
 > immune cells means there might be some immune
 > structures like tertiary lymphoid nodes or immune
 > networks.

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- **immune_median_distance_TSI_RCN.csv**

 - ![](media/image5.png){width="5.322331583552056in"
 > height="3.0364588801399823in"}

 - ![](media/image13.png){width="5.380208880139983in"
 > height="3.5868055555555554in"}

ma_ratio** (if high ratio -\> more tumor, this is a
 > bad condition) **& tumor_stroma_interface_length** (tell you
 > the shape of the tumor)

 - If low ratio -\> high OS or PFS

 - Tumor_stroma_interface -\> we are not sure with OS or PFS.
 > Could be hard to normalize this value (Have a try!)

- Allimage_spatial_metric_2 **(low priority to integrate)**

 - For **each cell type**：(we don't sure which cell type will
 > contribute more -\> if you are interested in it, you can start
 > with [B cells and CD4T cells]{.underline})

 - **NND:** nearest neighbour distance, measure the
 > distribution of certain cell type, are they loose or
 > dense.

 - **cluster Density:** compute the density of clusters with
 > DBSCAN, radius = 30px, minimum of 10 cells are considered
 > as a cluster.

 - **Gini Density:** Gini coefficient for cell density
 > variation, measure the inequality of a distribution.

 - More close to 1 means the more unequal the distribution
 > of certain cell type. e.g. The unequal distribution of
 > immune cells means there might be some immune
 > structures like tertiary lymphoid nodes or immune
 > networks.

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- **immune_median_distance_TSI_RCN.csv**

 - ![](media/image5.png){width="5.322331583552056in"
 > height="3.0364588801399823in"}

 - ![](media/image13.png){width="5.380208880139983in"
 > height="3.5868055555555554in"}

*

 - ![](media/image5.png){width="5.322331583552056in"
 > height="3.0364588801399823in"}

 - ![](media/image13.png){width="5.380208880139983in"
 > height="3.5868055555555554in"}

 - ![](media/image6.png){width="5.372343613298337in"
 > height="3.5781255468066493in"}

 - How to do the analysis:

 - You pick a cell type (NK, Treg, CD4T or CD8T) and integrate
 > the value in your cox model

 - Expectation: lower immune -\> TSI distance -\> longer OS or
 > PFS.

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- Immunenetwork_delaunay.csv (lymphonet or myelonet)

 - Feature: **avg_edge_length**

 - Expectation: lower length might mean more dense immune cell
 > distribution, thus better OS or PFS

**In total 7 features for Cox model.**

## 5.5.2025

### Update from Venla

The forest plots and the Cox model codes are working well - the next
step is to control the quality of the data.

### Plan for full time

For the data preparation and quality - [future plan (for full
time)]{.underline}

[Feature Engineering!]{.underline}

1. **[If some variables are highly]{.underline}**
 > **[relevant]{.underline} \* \~ 1 month**

 a. **The goal is to create a pipeline to evaluate the similarity or
 > correlations between the variables.**

 b. Give suggestions to ppl which variables to keep and which to
 > drop.

 c. Some materials

*

 - ![](media/image5.png){width="5.322331583552056in"
 > height="3.0364588801399823in"}

 - ![](media/image13.png){width="5.380208880139983in"
 > height="3.5868055555555554in"}

 - ![](media/image6.png){width="5.372343613298337in"
 > height="3.5781255468066493in"}

 - How to do the analysis:

 - You pick a cell type (NK, Treg, CD4T or CD8T) and integrate
 > the value in your cox model

 - Expectation: lower immune -\> TSI distance -\> longer OS or
 > PFS.

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- Immunenetwork_delaunay.csv (lymphonet or myelonet)

 - Feature: **avg_edge_length**

 - Expectation: lower length might mean more dense immune cell
 > distribution, thus better OS or PFS

**In total 7 features for Cox model.**

## 20.5.2025

Some notes before our meeting by Ziqi

More details about the goal:

Build a reproducible pipeline to assess relevance, redundancy, and
optimal encoding of features from clinical + spatial analysis +
molecular profile. In the future

**Correlation Analysis**: Pearson/Spearman/Kendall for continuous,
Cramér's V or mutual information for categorical variables. ...

**Redundancy Check**: Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) and pairwise
heatmaps to prune highly collinear variables. **Encoding strategies**:
Bin continuous variables based on domain thresholds (e.g., the long &
short PFS definition; tumor purity(H&E, not sure if we need this) \< 0.5
as low). \-\-- There are many detailed small work to do...

iMTB report coming soon!

normalized patient-level features (HRD, PFI, purity, expression changes,
CNV, etc.) Outcome - visualize archetype movement and convergence.

similar to published works (see following).

height="2.9809295713035873in"}

## 11.08.2025

### Ziqi: [rerun the SPACEstat analysis]{.underline} for all slides and save all spatial metrics

These following tasks aim to replicate and extend spatial TME analyses
similar to published works (see following).

The goal is to assess how tumor--stroma interface (TSI) features,
spatial cell organization, and genomic alterations relate to overall
survival (OS) and tumor phenotypes.

Example from
[[https://www.nature.com/articles/s41590-024-01943-5]{.underline}](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41590-024-01943-5)

![](media/image14.png){width="6.389583333333333in"
height="4.488456911636045in"}

Example from Fer's preprint "Single-cell spatial atlas of high-grade
serous ovarian cancer uncovers MHC class II as a key predictor of
spatial tumor ecosystems and clinical outcomes":

![](media/image18.png){width="3.5229166666666667in"
height="2.9809295713035873in"}

### To-do list

- **For the datasets you have already used**

 - **RCNs.**

 - What are RCNs? Spatial clusters of cell neighbourhoods
 > frequently occurring across samples. Certain RCNs may be

- **For the datasets you have already used**

 - **RCNs.**

 - What are RCNs? Spatial clusters of cell neighbourhoods
 > frequently occurring across samples. Certain RCNs may be
 > enriched in specific molecular profiles (e.g., HRP) or
 > linked to survival.

 - **Goal:** Identify RCNs enriched in HRP and evaluate their
 > association with OS.

 - **Action:**

 - Focus first on RCNs 3, 9, 11 (HRP-enriched in previous
 > results). Also examine TSI-related RCNs: 10, 12, and
 > 6.

 - Test their impact on OS using a Cox proportional hazards
 > model. Interpret HR \> 1 as increased hazard (worse
 > prognosis), HR \< 1 as protective.

 - ![](media/image1.png){width="2.776042213473316in"
 > height="2.6761843832021in"}

 > CNA → "Not_detected" if all three genes are Not_detected,
 > otherwise "detected".

 > signature.

 > alternative grouping.

 - **Cell_type_basic_metrics.csv,** contains NND, Cluster density,
 > and Gini Density for each cell type in each slide.

 - Higher NND suggests that the tumor cells are more spread out
 > or less clustered. Higher Cluster Density suggests that
 > tumor cells are forming denser clusters. Higher Gini
 > density suggests that the tumor cells are unevenly
 > distributed across the area.

 - **Goal:** See if tumor cell spatial organization influences
 > OS.

 - **Action:** Fit a Cox model for OS using these metrics. Pay
 > special attention to tumor cell metrics; optionally check
 > immune/stroma cells.

 - **TSI_metrics.csv** contains the global immune-TSI (tumor-stroma
 > interface) proximity.

 - I suggest: Cox model for OS to test if closer immune--TSI
 > proximity predicts survival.

 - **Immune_infiltration_index.csv** contains the immune
 > infiltration index (calculated by immune density in tumor
 > region / immune density in stroma region)

 - I suggest: Cox model to see if high infiltration index
 > predicts OS. Also, you can divide the infiltration as
 > low/median/high and plot e.g.

 - ![](media/image11.png){width="4.563708442694663in"
 > height="2.818486439195101in"}

 - **immune_TSI distances.csv** contains the global median distance
 > between each type of immune cells to TSI per slide.

 - For each immune cell type

 - Use the median distance per patient as a continuous
 > variable in a Cox proportional hazards model for OS.

 - Also, stratify into tertiles (low, medium, high
 > proximity) and plot Kaplan--Meier curves. Report
 > hazard ratios (HR), 95% CI, and p-values.

 > proximity is significantly associated with survival.\
 > Summarize findings in a forest plot (one HR per immune
 > cell type).

 > by local residency (tumor-resident vs stroma-resident immune
